description: This schematic diagram (Fig. 12) illustrates the micromechanism of dislocation-mediated slip reversibility in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel under two processing conditions. Panel (a) depicts the solution-treated (ST) condition where short-range ordering (SRO) within the austenitic matrix facilitates initial planar slip via glide plane softening; however, the weak and spatially heterogeneous SRO structure undergoes premature destruction under repeated dislocation shearing, resulting in localized strain concentrations and low slip reversibility. Panel (b) contrasts the aged condition, where coherent ordered L1₂-structured intragranular κ-carbides provide stable obstacles for dislocation shearing. The formation of anti-phase boundaries (APBs) during initial cutting reduces resistance for subsequent dislocations on the same planes, channeling slip activity to well-defined softened planes, restricting cross-slip, and enabling dislocations to retrace their paths during load reversal. This mechanism promotes homogeneous planar slip band formation and significantly enhances slip reversibility, rationalizing the superior fatigue performance of aged samples compared to ST samples.
